desc:
The large road runs from the east to the west. Occasional animal noises can be
heard from the bushes that grow by the side of the road. To the north some water
can be seen glimmering in the distance.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The large road is cutting through the hills like a sharp knife; whoever it was
that built it must have been very good at mining. Some tufts of grass grow on
the road and some trees grow beside it. The road bends west and north here.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
An offshoot of the small stream to the west covers much of the road here with a
few inches of water that are easily waded through. Perhaps someday someone might
build a bridge over this piece of land if the water level rises and it becomes a
hinderance to passage along this often travelled road. Following the road will
take you east or south.
name: Small Stream Crossing

desc:
Along the road some small trees grow providing shade for the tired travellers
that pass this way. In the north and south the great Tower Hills reach for the
sky. The road continues to the north and west.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Filled with clear, cold water this small lake constitutes the border between the
high hills to the north and the lower plains to the south. Some small fish can
be seen swimming just above the rock-covered bottom. A small stream flows out of
the lake to the north.
name: A Small Lake

desc:
This lake is filled with clear, cold water. Some small fish can be seen in the
clear water near the bottom and many water insects assemble on the surface of
the lake, attracting fish and occasional birds. Thick bushes and low trees cover
the shores. A small river flows out of the lake to the east. A high and mighty
cliff rises towards the sky to the north.
name: A Small Lake

desc:
A small river appears from a wide fissure in the ground, follows the bends of
the rocky slope and continue in a fast pace due west. Just after the river comes
out of the underground, a small stream leaves the rocky channel and continues
east. The banks are filled with lush, green grass and small bushes. To the south
a large road passes by and to the north the great Tower Hills can be seen.
name: Small River

desc:
Here the cavern comes to an abrupt end, the uneven stone walls blocking passage
in every direction but west. An old rotten cupboard hangs on the wall, its doors
attached by one hinge each. Someone apparently uses this place as a refuse dump
as food leftovers are scattered all around the room. The stench here is almost
unbearable.
name: Dead-End

desc:
The cavern is filled with all types of junk. Broken furniture and rotten
clothing are strewn all around. The floor is soft and damp from the water that
trickles down the stone walls, assembling in small pools on the floor. A fragile
ladder leads up to a higher level of the cavern and there are exits to the east
and west.
name: Rocky Cavern

desc:
This part of the cavern is quite nice and tidy, as if somebody actually cares
how it looks. Torches light the room with a flickering light, and a carpet
covers the uneven floor. A table with some chairs placed around it stands in the
middle of the room. To the north there is another lit room, but to the east the
cavern extends into darkness.
name: Guard Room

desc:
Lit by the many torches hanging on the walls, this part of the cavern is
actually quite comfortable. The air is dry and warm, and the floor is covered by
a large grizzly bear fur, complete with head and paws. A large oaken table with
five chairs around it stands along one of the walls. On the western wall you can
see a crude oil painting of a high tower.
name: Smugglers' Hideout

desc:
The air here is drier than in the cave to the south and permeated by the stale
smell of old, rotten food supplies. In a corner of the cavern some coffins are
stacked on top of each other. To the south the cavern continues. A fragile
ladder leads down.
name: Rocky Cavern

desc:
The floor of this cave is covered with hard, sandy soil. Small rivulets of water
trickle down the stone walls, forming pools of water on the floor. A narrow
ladder leads up to the roof. To the north the cave continues further into the
hill.
name: Entrance to a Cave

desc:
This huge old hill stands here like a wedge, forcing the road to the Grey Havens
to take a turn around it. East and south of here the ancient Tower Hills stretch
out, while travelling north and west would take you down to the road.
name: Hill Overlooking the Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Large green bushes grow on the hilltops, breaking the monotony of the otherwise
mostly grass and weed-covered hills. Some large boulders are scattered among the
bushes. The hills continue in all directions, but an old road winds its way
through the hills to the north and east.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
These gentle rolling green hills are overlooking the Road to the Grey Havens
that meanders through the landscape to your north and west. East of here the
landscape gradually changes into a more dramatic view: that must be the famous
Tower Hills.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
These hills differ markedly from the grassy hills that can be seen to your west.
There is virtually no vegetation here, only some thorny bushes and patches of
high yellow grass. The hills themselves seem old and grey, naked rocks
interspersed with eroded cliffs.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
From this rocky hill, more rocky hills can be seen in all directions. There is
little wildlife and flora here. Despite the desolate appearance of the land,
tough animals and hardy vegetation can obviously make a living in this area. To
the west the landscape get more cultivated and easier to travel through.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
Here one can find rock, stones, cliffs and more rocks. There is absolutely no
vegetation here. The hills are completely naked and exposed to the erosive
forces of sun, wind and rain. On one of the hills one can see the remains of a
campfire.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
These towering hills look very dramatic indeed. Comprised of black and dark-grey
rocks, they loom threateningly over any mounted traveller. One of the hills
looks particularly menacing, and it takes just about no imagination to make out
a grinning face in the rock. Travelling west towards some greener hills seems
easier than to venture further into the towering hills north and east of here.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The hills continue in all directions. Travel southwards from here is prevented
by a sheer rock face, but one can venture out among the hills in all other
directions. The hills north and west of here seem gentler and greener, while the
hills to your east are naked rock.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Various types of grasses cover these old hills. On the southern slopes one can
see a type of herb that is unusual to find around these parts of Arda: a tough,
grey-green plant with sticky yellowish leaves. Looking up from these flowery
distractions one can discern the road to the Grey Havens to the north.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Among these grass-covered hills stands a white weather-worn rock. Upon closer
inspection one can see that it must have been erected here for some purpose, but
it is unclear what this purpose might have been. In the shade of this rock grows
a tough birch that seems to be older than the hill itself.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Here lie the remains of what once must have been some sort of roadside hut.
Decaying logs and patches of gravel tell a story of some sort, but there are few
other signs of habitation here. It is possible to travel north to the road
between the Grey Havens and the eastern lands. One can also venture among the
hills east and west of here.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
A plethora of different grasses covers these ancient hills. The dominant type
seems to be a tough, low grey-green weed. However, what catches the eye are the
patches of a tall red-leafed grass that reaches almost four feet here and there.
North of here runs the road between the Grey Havens and the eastern lands.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
This is a fairly large grass-covered hill. From this spot one has a fair view of
the surrounding area. North of here is the road to Grey Havens. To the south and
east a landscape of rolling hills stretches out. A vast grassy plain lies to the
west. There is something solemn and untouched about this place.
name: Grassy Hill

desc:
The ground is covered by grass and small bushes with only an occasional tree
sometimes breaking the monotony. To the east a large grassy hill rises above the
land, but to the north and west the grassy plains continue.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Vast grassy plains stretch out before you with small trees and thorny bushes
scattered all across the open landscape. In the far east large hills fill the
horizon and to the west cultivated land takes over.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The landscape ripples with amber waves of grain. The wheat appears ripe and must
be ready to be harvested soon. Gently swaying stalks continue towards the west.
Further north the golden field gives way to a large area of corn. No trees are
in sight and the landscape seems quite flat.
name: A Golden Field

desc:
Densely packed wheat grows here giving this whole area a rich golden colour.
Some small paths lead through the field, probably the remnants of the farmers
movements when this field was sown. Some of the stalks appear to have been
nibbled on by a small creature. The field continues eastward but passage is
blocked by a fence in the west.
name: A Golden Field

desc:
High stalks of corn rise all around, virtually blocking the view of the sky
above. A strong odour originates from the west, but a tall dark pile in that
direction blocks any investigation. The corn field continues towards the east,
while uneven ground leads northwards. Further south of here the tall green
stalks of corn give way to shorter yellow vegetation.
name: A Corn Field

desc:
Rows of corn grow here, their green stalks swaying in a gentle breeze. Though it
is difficult to see anything other than corn, there appears to be a field of
yellow foliage further south. North and east the corn gives way to shorter
vegetation. Whoever planted this corn must have spent a large amount of time
doing so, the rows are perfectly straight and evenly spaced.
name: A Corn Field

desc:
A small brook twists its way through the lush green fields here. Along the brook
some large bushes and small trees grow. To the north a large road passes by and
to the south the grassy plains continue while to the west cultivated land takes
over the wild plains.
name: By a Small Brook

desc:
Soft lush grass covers the level ground. Some small, thorny bushes provide
shelter for the small animals that live in these fertile plains. To the east and
north a large road passes by and to the west and south the plains continue.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The uneven ground here makes travel difficult. The ground has been forcefully
dug up everywhere and turned over so as to bring the nutrient rich dark earth to
the surface. Only a few spots of grass are still visible, their numbers
increasing further in the west. Southwards high stalks of corn grow while to the
north flat, well-travelled land indicates the presence of a road.
name: A Ploughed Field

desc:
Little vegetation grows here, the ground has been recently turned over exposing
some worms and dark earth. Hoofprints along with bootprints can be seen along
the uneven ground. Directly north the flat earth indicates a more frequently
travelled area. Turned over earth continues in a western direction while high
stalks partially hinder your travel southwards.
name: A Ploughed Field

desc:
Patches of vibrant yellow flowers cover the area, interspersed among a variety
of long grasses. Cart tracks lead north towards a well travelled road and
westwards. A continuous buzzing of numerous insects prevails and an occasional
butterfly flutters past you.
name: A Faded Path

desc:
Indentations in the ground along with flattened vegetation indicate a path
extending east and west. This area seems well travelled by heavy carts and the
grass has recently been trimmed. South of here the rear side of a small
structure is visible while to the north a high fence blocks off a section of
field.
name: A Well-travelled Path

desc:
A thorny bush grows on the northern side of heavy cart tracks leading towards a
structure in the south. Similar tracks continue east along a path. Little grass
can be found here, the ground is almost totally covered with deep grooves from
heavy loads and frequent travel. A smaller less travelled path branches off from
the main tracks and leads west.
name: Bend in a Dirt Path

desc:
The small path bends here, leading north and eastwards. A high wall prevents
passage to the south while a building stands directly to the west. The path is
well worn but has been taken care of. No heavy animal tracks or machinery pass
through here leaving the ground solid and covered in small flowers.
name: Bend in a Small Path

desc:
This area is sheltered on all sides except east. The ground is covered by small
rocks and some straw and widens to the north. South a door blocks entrance to a
hay-filled stable while a heavy wooden door leads west into a farmhouse. Iron
rings embedded in the southern wall must serve as a place to tie up livestock.
No roof covers this area and gusts of wind occasionally stir up a few haulms of
corn. A covered passage can be entered east of here.
name: An Open Courtyard

desc:
Barn doors stand to the north and south. On the walls beside the doors hang
various halters and leather pieces. The short passage continues west into an
open courtyard and east to the outside of this barn. The roof is in need of
repair as the sky can be seen through a gaping hole.
name: A Covered Passage

desc:
The entire enclosure is filled with loose hay. A path has been created heading
north and south between a door and the other end of the barn. Outside sounds are
muffled in here except for occasional wind howling through cracks in the walls
and stirring up pieces of hay.
name: Hay Storage

desc:
The wood on the door leading into this stable has been chewed at by something
with a lot of enthusiasm. In fact, much of the room has teeth marks on bits of
the wood. The floor is covered with straw and a strong equine odour prevails.
name: A Small Stable

desc:
A partial roof has been built here to allow for shelter from the elements. The
ground is trampled and soiled by heavy livestock crowding in this area. A few
helms of hay suggest this to be a feeding area as well. A wooden trough stands
underneath the covering providing a source of water. A fence prevents animals
from leaving in all directions save east.
name: By a Shelter

desc:
The grass here is short and in sharp contrast to some yellow flowers that remain
untouched by the grazing of the livestock in this pasture. A fence prevents
animals from escaping to the south or north. Circular patches cover the ground
in spots and seem to attract a variety of insects.
name: A Green Pasture

desc:
The earth has been dug up here by frequent passage of livestock and carts. A
barn stands directly to the west with a small passage leading through it towards
a farmhouse. Various farming equipment along with a battered cart have been left
next to the southwestern barnwall. A gate blocks livestock from leaving their
southern pasture and a very small building can be spotted to the east.
name: Outside a Barn

desc:
A wooden fence consisting of a number of planks prevents animals from leaving
this area to the east or south. An unmistakable odour drifts here from the
north. Grass grows in a variety of shades and heights.
name: A Green Pasture

desc:
Steaming piles of manure have been heaped almost to the height of a full grown
elf. A path leads up through the juicy mess and a dirty bucket next to the
manure pile suggests someone has the daily chore of adding more manure to the
heap. Various insects can be seen scuttling among the mass enjoying their feast.
name: By a Manure Pile

desc:
This room is very small and seems to have been constructed for a single purpose.
A long wooden box spans the entire eastern wall. The box is shaped strangely and
appears to have been created out of two pieces. The pieces stand at different
heights and both have a slab of wood covering a hole leading down into the
hollow box. A small bucket of water stands next to the box and a number of cloth
strips hang on the northern wall.
name: An Outhouse

desc:
Several corpses of large wolf-like beasts lie on the floor, slowly decomposing.
The blood, once fresh, has dried and seeped into the hard earthen floor. Corners
of the low ceiling are covered with thick, dusty cobwebs.
name: Scarred Pit

desc:
A barely noticeable footpath runs across the valley, bending around the large
stones. Debris from rock and rotten trees blocks the passage to the grass-
covered hill in the northern part of the valley. Grass and occasional
wildflowers cover the soil, with a few rocks scattered around. A wall of high
bushes grows between the two hills in the east.
name: Small Hills

desc:
This stone-covered slope slowly declines towards a small valley to the north.
Some sharp rocks lie about the worn footpath, which bends here and continues to
the east and to the south. A few small trees grow amongst the low, green bushes.
name: Small Hills

desc:
The ground is very soft and covered by some wild forest flowers. Pine trees loom
above a narrow path, which crosses the low hills. A burned-out circle of grass
marks a campfire which can be seen just west of a small trail junction nearby.
name: Forest Hills

desc:
These hills were once sharp and high, yet wind and rain has smoothed them.
Several trees grow on the rocky slopes, giving a perfect hideout to small
animals. Northwards, the forest becomes more deep and gloomy. A small footpath
cuts its way through several large stones.
name: Forest Hills

desc:
A small, rocky path comes down from the hilltop to the north and continues its
way to another group of hills to the south. The ground along the trail is quite
smooth and even, with some occasional rocks lying around. Several low birch
trees grow here and there along the footpath. The green grass of the plains
grows high, undisturbed for years.
name: The Small Path

desc:
A lot of tall pine trees grow nearby, dispersed along the slopes of the low
hills. A worn footpath runs between the pines and enters a green valley between
two large hills southwards. Moving to the east, the trees stand farther apart.
name: Forest Hills

desc:
Two huge, mossy rocks mark the entrance to this small valley between high hills.
A small, stone-paved trail heads north-south, avoiding the large rocks scattered
everywhere. The deep, green moss seems to thrive at the lowest levels of the
valley, leaving no room for other plants. Hill slopes to the east and west are
dominated by majestic trees and dense bushes.
name: Forest Hills

desc:
A stony path continues here, bordered by some small, moss-covered granite
pillars which are obviously man-made. The forest becomes more dense, with high,
ancient pines looming above the path. The surrounding hills and treetops block
all the sunlight during the daytime.
name: Small Hills

desc:
A stony trail leading from the north suddenly ends in a debris of rotten logs
and branches. Steep brush-covered hillsides make it impossible to travel in any
direction but to the north.
name: End of the Small Path

desc:
A small underground stream runs here, coming from the north and disappearing
into a small hole in the southern cave wall. The air in here is very moist and
hard to breathe. Some small pieces of a skeleton are lying in a corner, and
along the walls stand several large stone coffins. The lids of most of the
coffins are broken and the contents appear to have been stolen. The cave
continues to the west and north.
name: By an Underground Stream

desc:
The air is so damp here that it is hard to breathe. The water pouring down the
walls doesn't pool on the floor, but forms a small stream running to the south.
A skeleton of some sort of humanoid is propped up against the western wall.
Beside the skeleton the rusted remains of what appears to be plate armour lies
shattered on the floor. The cave continues to the north and south.
name: A Damp Cave

desc:
This cave is large and cold, the air is moist and the smell of rotten wood
strikes you as you enter. In the north-eastern corner some old coffins are left
to decay. The floor is covered by old animal droppings and down the walls small
rivulets of water are pouring down, forming small pools on the floor. A narrow
passage leads west and the cave continues further to the south.
name: A Damp Cave

desc:
The smooth, hard tunnel floor is damp and wet. The walls are covered by some
strange moss, softly glowing with some kind of fluorescent light. The light is
not bright enough to guide you in your travel but enough to make your journey a
bit less dark. The narrow tunnel continues to the east but to the north and west
the passage seems to be a bit larger and more comfortable.
name: A Narrow Passage

desc:
This part of the cave is dominated by one very large stalagmite. Small bits of a
skeleton lie scattered across the floor of the cave, making a cracking sound as
you walk over it. In the north-western corner two ancient stone coffins lie
propped against the wall. The air is dry and easy to breathe, and you can feel a
breeze of fresh air coming from a small crack in the northern wall.
name: Cave

desc:
Here the narrow tunnel splits three ways with one tunnel going off in an
east-west direction while a larger tunnel branches off to the north. The floor
is covered with small pointed rocks that make a strange cracking sound as you
walk across them. Looking closer, you realise that the rocks are in fact small
parts of a human skeleton.
name: A Tunnel Crossing

desc:
Low and narrow is this tunnel in the solid, rocky hill. Some small rivulets of
water pour down along the walls, making the soil on the floor moist and swampy.
The narrow tunnel continues to the east, but seems to open up into a larger cave
to the west. A large iron door is in the southern wall.
name: A Narrow Tunnel

desc:
A few broken coffins lie by the western wall of the cavern. The coffins are
empty, but many dry bones and skulls are scattered all over the floor as if
somebody had strewn them around while scavenging for valuables. A large sturdy
ladder leads up the southern wall. To the north and east the cavern extends into
darkness.
name: Grave Vault

desc:
The tunnel is quite narrow here. Small pools of water are continuously forming
on the ground, fed by tiny rivulets that trickle from the fine cracks in the
walls. The narrow tunnel continues to the north, but to the south it opens up
into a large cave.
name: A Narrow Tunnel

desc:
This tunnel is so narrow that any grown-up man would almost have to bow while
proceeding to avoid bumping his head against the tunnel roof. The floor is
covered with hard soil that is quite damp from the water that is pouring down
the walls. The tunnel continues east and south.
name: A Narrow Tunnel

desc:
Here the tunnel is very narrow and there is almost no room to manoeuvre. Sharp
pointy rocks protrude from the walls, tearing at your clothes whenever you move.
The air is moist from the many rivulets of water trickling down from the roof.
The tunnel continues to the east and west.
name: A Very Narrow Tunnel

desc:
Sharp rocks cover the floor, making passage somewhat hazardous. Equally sharp
rocks protrude from the walls which are covered with a net of cracks. Water is
pouring down these cracks, forming a small stream that disappears in a hole by
the base of the rocky wall to the east. The tunnel seems to be even narrower to
the west but opens up a bit to the south.
name: A Narrow Tunnel

desc:
In one of the corners of the cave some animal droppings can be seen; apparently
some animal uses this place as its resting quarters. The air is moist and hard
to breathe. The cave is quite big, its roof almost the height of two men. A
couple of torches hang on the walls, their flickering flames chasing away the
shadows. There is a narrow opening in the northern wall and two somewhat wider
openings in the eastern and the southern walls.
name: Small Cave

desc:
This tunnel is quite large, almost two men high and at least five men wide. The
floor is very smooth and slippery. The tunnel bends from the west to the south.
The walls are cracked and a smell of wet soil is prevalent in here.
name: A Large Stone Tunnel

desc:
This cave is more than four men in height, and almost circular. The floor is
quite flat, as if it has been trodden under many feet. Large torches are placed
on the walls a few feet apart from each other, lightening the room. In the
middle of the room a marble altar with a shrine is placed, and along the western
wall stand three large coffins.
name: Treasure Chamber

desc:
What must have at one time been a stable filled with fresh hay or straw has
become a stable filled with flies and some sort of bird droppings. A wooden bowl
lies knocked over in the middle of the small chamber, its contents of seeds
spilled onto the ground. In the north eastern corner stands a barrel filled with
murky water.
name: A Small Stall

desc:
The musty smell of a room long sealed from the outside greets anyone entering
this tunnel. Fine white stonework lines either wall of the tunnel, and the
stairs that lead down from above appear to have been hewn with care from white
stone. Southwards the tunnel vanishes in the darkness.
name: Entrance to the Old Tomb

desc:
Ancient Dwarven runes cover the walls of this large chamber. Five stone coffins,
also covered in Dwarven inscriptions, stand in line across the room. This tomb
must be the resting place of some very noble Dwarves. The coffins seem to have
been sealed by some forgotten Dwarven method, there appears to be no way into
them. The dark tunnel in the northern wall of the chamber seems to be the only
way out.
name: Tomb of the Dwarven Warriors

desc:
Golden lettering and pictures that sparkle in the light cover the walls of this
small tomb. This must be the resting place of someone very important. On closer
inspection, the lettering is not quite a true golden colour, but the initial
effect was stunning. The now familiar Dwarven runes, written in gold, cover most
of the stonework in the room.
name: Golden Tomb

desc:
A small path continues westwards, passing by wooden structures that lie both to
the north and south. The path consists of small rocks and has been virtually
cleared of grass. A border of yellow flowers has been planted along the path,
giving it a well groomed appearance. Further east the path seems to merge with a
more frequented road.
name: A Small Path

desc:
Fine white sand without the presence of rocks covers the ground. The sand has
been blown up into piles to the north and east where solid wooden buildings
block off the area. This location may have served a purpose some time ago, but
right now it appears to be only a place where mounds of sand collects. A larger
sandy area opens up to the west.
name: A Sand Pit

desc:
This is a wild garden in which aromatic herbs of all kinds as well as flowers
grow in patches. Small paths snake through the grass, which also has been cut
back near the patches were the planted vegetation grows. To the south, the
garden ends at a houses's wall.
name: A Garden of Herbs and Flowers

desc:
This small building is quite rickety and the roof contains numerous holes and
cracks. Many wooden planks of all sizes and shapes lie scattered on the floor,
which itself is covered in splinters and sawdust. In the centre of the room, the
beginnings of a small boat seem to be taking form, yet it is not even close to
completion.
name: A Shack

desc:
This has been, decades or even centuries past, a quarry where stones got broken.
Even though the soil is sandy, thin bushes have taken over where once people
toiled and their roots found grip in even the tiniest crack in a stone. Towards
the east the area becomes more moist and the vegetation denser until the view is
blocked by a building, which roof is overgrown with vines.
name: An Overgrown Quarry

desc:
Lush pastures cover these fields, and the grass and bushes show clear signs of
grazing livestock. Tall, yellow grass grows in scattered tufts in some
inaccessible places near stone walls and wooden fences. Smoke rises from the
chimney of a nearby farmhouse, dancing as it rises towards the skies. A sturdy
fence encloses a wheat field that sways gently in the wind.
name: Pasture

desc:
Rows of carrots, potatoes, and other vegetables grow on this field, irrigated by
an intricate system of wooden channels. A water tank, partially propped up by a
wooden fence at the edge of the field to the south, supplies the irrigation
channels with necessary water for the otherwise dry ground. The fields are
bordered by a bush-covered area to the north. A farm lies beyond the fence,
where the farmers that tend to this field most likely live.
name: Vegetable Fields

desc:
The ground is flat and covered by sand. The sand is very loose and deep,
consisting entirely of small, white grains. Any tracks should be easily visible
here unless swept away by the wind. There is a farm visible far to the
north-east, beyond the sands. Small clumps of bushes and yellow grass grow
around these sandy fields.
name: Edge of Sandy Fields

desc:
This countryside forms a gentle transition between white, sandy plains to
northwest and verdant cultivated fields to the southeast. Grass grows in
abundance, its colour increasingly green and vibrant for each mile to the south
and east. A dense, impassable wall of bushes grows on the northern border of
these fields.
name: Edge of Grassy Fields

desc:
Winds sweep over undulating waves of a grassy sea. The hue of the grass varies
from pale yellow to deep green depending on the availability of moisture. The
fields continue to the west and east, while dense bushes surrounded by high
grass prevent any travel and block all view to the north. A small hill stands to
the south, fruit trees covering its gentle slopes. A bushy hedge at the foot of
hill protects an orchard from wild animals.
name: Grassy Fields under a Hill

desc:
A belt of grassland runs east to west, separating wild, dense brush to the north
from an orchard to the south. The grass grows tall and vigorous, and swathes of
it have been cut down, likely for hay. Scattered trees grow here and there. A
steep embankment topped with a bushy hedge protects the orchard to the south
from trespassing animals.
name: Grassy Fields by an Orchard

desc:
Rows of potatoes as well as patches of carrots, parsley, celery, and tomatoes
can be found in these carefully cultivated fields. Narrow paths link these
patches to a small central well. There is a fence at the northern border of the
fields, protecting the vegetables from roving livestock beyond the enclosure.
name: Vegetable Fields by a Fence

desc:
Narrow paths weave between numerous vegetable patches. A few baulks covered in
grass and bushes break up the monotony of this otherwise level land. A wooden
fence separates these fields from pastures to the north. There is a small barn
to the immediate east, surrounded on all sides by varied crops. A wooden winch
over a well stands close to the barn, serving as a water supply.
name: Vegetable Fields

desc:
Vast fields cultivated by local farmers can be seen in all directions. Corn
seems to be the most common cereal grown here. The soil is humid, loose, and
fertile. These fields can feed a large population when cared for properly. A
wooden fence borders the northern end of the fields, protecting them against
foraging livestock. A small barn stands in the western end of the fields.
name: Golden Field by a Barn

desc:
Vast fields expand in all directions, disturbed only by tiny, well-maintained
trails made by the farmers who are cultivating this area. Mostly corn is grown
here, creating a golden sea waving in the wind. The soil is humid, loose and
fertile. These fields can feed a large population when cared for properly.
name: Golden Field

desc:
This field has been thoroughly tilled; it is free of rocks and weeds. An
irrigation system composed of small wooden channels enables the soil to host a
generous supply of root vegetables. The vegetable field continues to the west,
and a fence spans the eastern side. A wicket in the fence stands at the foot of
a small hill covered with an orchard.
name: Root Vegetable Field

desc:
Numerous fruit trees spread their broad crowns high above the ground, their
roots delving deep into soft, rich soil. These trees appear to bear chiefly
apple or plum, interspersed here and there with varieties of peach. A cluster of
small buildings punctuates the western horizon. A fence made of wooden stakes
runs along the southern edge of the orchard. There is a small wicket in the
middle of the fence.
name: Orchard near the Farm

desc:
The deep green grass in this fertile pasture grows several inches high,
providing a source of food for an entire village's livestock during the summer
months. The grass has been grazed almost to the earth in some areas. A fence
separates the pasture from the fields to the west and to the north. A few
buildings stand at the north-west corner of the fence: a large wooden barn, a
stable, and a farmhouse.
name: Pasture by a Farm

desc:
A tall cliff, towering atleast twenty feet, leaves this part of the orchard in
shade for most of the day. For this reason, only bushes and shrubs that require
sparse sunlight grow here, while the fruit trees flourish in the western parts
of the orchard.
name: Shaded Orchard under a Cliff

desc:
An orchard covers the side of a hill, gradually climbing up a gentle slope. The
fruit trees are well tended and planted in rows, with a tree every ten to twenty
feet. Atop the centre of the hill benches made of half-logs sit around the
remains of a campfire. It appears the local elves frequently visit this place.
name: Hill Overlooking an Orchard

desc:
This eastern section of a large orchard is less crowded than the western part.
Hard, rocky soil contributes to the sparsity of trees, as well as to their
diminished stature. However, the trees are still able to grow deep, healthy
roots and produce delicious, though smaller, fruit. A fence encloses the
orchard, and there is a gate to the south.
name: Sparse Orchard

desc:
Green and healthy-looking fruit bushes grow scattered across the fields. The
oocasional rabbit can be seen bounding between bushes, looking for fallen fruit
or tasty leaves. A few trees dot the landscape, mostly sprawling oaks standing
in lone majesty. These seem ideal places for squirrels to hide nuts and berries
for the inevitable winter.
name: Wild Fruit Bushes

desc:
A few scattered trees grow among small brushy plants below a bluff. The bluff
towers steeply to the east, and lacks any obvious way up. Tracks of small
animals lead to and from rodent-sized holes punctuating uncultivated ground.
However, there are fields of wheat are visible beyond a fence to the north.
name: Fields beneath the Bluffs

desc:
The land here is flat, without any distinct features. Cultivated fields of corn
extend in all directions, making this area a bread basket for a big population.
However, not far to the south the fields are bordered by a wall of small thorny
bushes. Hills rise a few miles beyond these bushes.
name: Golden Field

desc:
The land changes shape toward the southeast as the fields give way to hills.
This field, part of a large area of cornfields that extend towards the
north-west, is bordered by a dense brush to the south and east and a high fence
separates the field from the wilderness. The fence also protects the fields from
foraging deer and sheep. There is a small wicket in the fence, through which the
local elves can comfortably access the wilderness beyond.
name: Golden Field by a Fence

desc:
Fertile fields cultivated by the local Elven population spread far to the east
and north. The fields are cut off by a wide depression to the south, and a hill
to the west where an orchard climbs the slope. A small farm stands at the foot
of the orchard. The slopes of the depression are steep and loose and make
descending it too dangerous to attempt.
name: Golden Fields below an Orchard

desc:
Rows of apple and cherry trees climb a hillside that rises slowly to the west.
Trees alternate with patches of cultivated flowers. There are three wooden
benches at the top of hill; one faces the orchard, and the other two are
oriented in the direction of the gulf. To the west, at the foot of the hill,
there is a dense grove filled with bushes. A small farmhouse stands at the
eastern end of the orchard.
name: Orchard

desc:
A vast, flat plain stretches away to the west. This grassy peninsula is bordered
by dense rows of bushes to the south, a small orchard atop a hill to the north,
and a broad depression to the east. Scattered dung and hoofprints reveal that
this grassland is used for pasture.
name: Pasture

desc:
This wide, flat depression is roughly six feet deep and was likely formed by
erosion. The soil is soggy as water constantly flows down from higher ground in
all directions. Shallow pools of standing water on the bottom of the depression,
surrounded by mud and sedges, attract insects and small birds. The sides of the
depression are very steep to the south and north, but with some effort it is
possible to climb out of it to the west and east.
name: Watery Depression

desc:
Clumps of bushes and high grass cover these gently rolling mounds. In some
areas, the bushes form large areas of dense brush, dozens of yards across and
completely impassable. From the tops of the highest mounds, a small valley is
visible to the south, nestled among the foothills of the Tower Hills. As the
land rises to the south, the vegetation becomes sparser. A depression, water
glinting in pools at its bottom, lies to the west.
name: Bushy Mounds

desc:
Vibrant grass, flowers, and clover blanket a large, open pasture. The grass is
fairly short, with only scattered blades exceeding half a foot in height. Dried
dung and numerous tracks suggest that extensive grazing is responsible for
keeping the grass in check. The pastureland continues in every direction but
north, where it meets a broad swath of brush.
name: Pasture

desc:
This square court is well maintained with a floor made out of grass and
flagstone. It is surrounded with a long living house on the one side, a barn in
the front, and a stable opposite the house. A six feet high stone wall completes
the enclosure. A hay wagon stands close to the barn and the noise of animals can
be heard through the open doors.
name: Farm Courtyard

desc:
This dense brushland is composed mostly of interwoven bushes and tall grass,
though a few short trees rise above the other vegetation. Patches of bushes
become increasingly sparse to the north and south as the brushland fades into
the neighbouring fields. However, to the east the brushland becomes dense to the
point of being impassable at the base of a small hill. A solid fence protects an
orchard to the west.
name: Bushy Belt at a Base of a Small Hill

desc:
The grass here has been heavily grazed, and there are small pools of mud where
it has been trampled into the ground. Cattle tracks lead to and from green
pastures to the south and east. A wooden fence on the northern and western sides
of this part of the pasture protect an orchard from the roving bovines. A gate
in the fence to the north allows access to the orchard from this direction.
name: Pasture by an Orchard

desc:
The land is rockier here than in the grass-covered fields to the north. Small
mounds of rocks and gravel rise a few feet above the surrounding plain. Isolated
clumps of bushes have found soil to take root in, while the rest of the area is
sparsely vegetated. A wall built from stones piled atop each other separates
this land from whatever lies to the south.
name: Scrubland

desc:
High, dense grass covers these undulating plains. Clumps of of bushes grow in
small dells and break the monotony of the green ocean. Bypassing these dells
makes travelling slow and difficult. Just to the north bushes form a long belt
going from the east to the west. Foothills rise to the south-east, as the Tower
Hills lie in that direction. There is a well-maintained wooden fence to the
west, protecting cornfields beyond it. A small wicket in the fence allows access
to the fields beyond the fence.
name: Bushy Fields

desc:
Dense isolated islands of wide, thorny bushes grow in the high grass that covers
these hills. Towards the west they form a wall that looks impenetrable. The land
rises further to the south-east, where the foothills become the Tower Hills
proper. Tracks of wildlife can be seen in several places and grass and bushes
alike show sign of being set upon by herbivores.
name: Bushy Foothills

desc:
A few hills form a narrow ridge slowly rising from north to south. The slopes of
these hills are steep and covered with small shrubs. A descent looks to be a
feat beyond mere mortals. A narrow, barely visible trail climbs the ridge
towards higher peaks to the south. There is a small valley at the foot of the
slopes to the west.
name: Narrow Ridge

desc:
Steep slopes to the east and south create a small valley here. The valley's
bottom is covered with grass nurtured by a small creek originating from a spring
at the foot of the eastern slope. There is a wooden hut with a bench close to
this spring. It is a quiet place where one can rest, if needed. It seems that it
is possible to climb the southern slopes up to a low ridge.
name: Valley in the Tower Hills

desc:
Gently rolling plains extend in all directions. High grass covers the ground,
suitable as forage for livestock. Someone recently scythed a large area to the
west, and a few haystacks stand there. The plains turn into hillocks to the
south, and a small valley leads into the foothills of the Tower Hills to the
east. A steep, long scarp borders the plains to the north, blocking any way in
this direction.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Vibrant green grass thrives in these fertile plains. Scattered bundles of hay
have been left to dry in the centre of swaths of cleared grass. Shallow wagon
ruts lead west, and tracks of livestock lead south into a fenced pasture. A wall
of low bushes runs along the northern end of this grassland.
name: Green Grassland

desc:
Verdant, level pasture extends for miles in all directions. Immediately to the
south there is a small hill. Beyond it to the south and east, the plains begin
to undulate as they approach the Tower Hills. The vegetation here is tall, and
seems to see little grazing. However, clear swathes cut across the land where
grass has been gathered for use as hay.
name: Pasture

desc:
Green pastures stretch in all directions. The grass is tall and luscious,
delicious grazing for wandering livestock. To the west a giant rock slab towers
over the grasslands. Alhough it is possible to climb up this side of the rock,
it would probably be better to ascend the southern slope, where the side of the
rock is not so steep. There is little else to catch the eye in this endless sea
of grass.
name: Pasture

desc:
Endless flat plains extend to the west at the foot of these hillocks, and the
horizon to the south-east is dominated by the Tower Hills. The hills are covered
with grass, with a few bushes dotting the slopes. A large but lonely pasture to
the west, enclosed by a sturdy wooden fence, is the only sign of habitation.
name: Grassy Hillocks

desc:
Low hummocks begin to rise out of grassy plains, increasing in height to the
south and east. A few bushes grow in shallow vales between the mounds, but grass
still covers most of the ground. The grass flourishes, and will no doubt provide
excellent grazing for livestock. A steep slope towers to the west, where a ridge
ascends to a bluff.
name: Southern End of a Pasture

desc:
A small hill rises from the surrounding plains, providing a clear view of
distant features lying beyond the miles of grassland. A fenced pasture lies
immediately to the east, with the summits of the Tower Hills forming a distant
backdrop. Trampled swaths of grass reveal the paths trod by cattle roving
between this hilltop and pastures to the north.
name: Round Hummock

desc:
Luscious, green grass carpets the ground, stretching away south and east as far
as the eye can see. The rich and fertile soil supports diverse plant life; small
clusters of daisies and buttercups grow among the grass. Low mounds covered in
brush rise to the west.
name: Fertile Plains

desc:
Rolling hills blanketed in brush run from the north to the south where the
ground suddenly dips into a small depression. The mounds are high enough so that
a clear view of the landscape is only visible from their summits. Paw prints and
wolf-droppings suggest that a pack of wolves has moved into this area, feasting
on the rodents dwelling in the underbrush as well as livestock in the
surrounding pastures.
name: Brush-covered Mounds

desc:
A trail that crosses these meadows ends at the foot of a rising ridge to the
north. The ridge is barren and stony; if the travellers who made the trail also
regularly ascend the ridge, there is no sign of it. The grass is green and seems
to thrive in the wet lowland soil. Flowers aplenty dot the meadow, and the humm
and buzz of insects is ever present.
name: Meadows at the Foot of a Rocky Ridge

desc:
These fields lie in the shadows of hills rising to the southeast. The grassland
provides grazing for livestock when adverse weather forces them down from the
hills. A fence runs along the northern side of the pasture, perhaps built to
protect the grazers of this pasture from the threat of predators. A few patches
of white wool are caught in the fence.
name: Grazing Pasture

desc:
The grass grows tall on this gentle slope. The land continues to climb into the
hills to the east, and to the west the foot of the slope meets a seemingly
endless plain. An animal track is discernible among the grass, leading south
along the slope.
name: End of a Faint Trail

desc:
Gently rolling pastures stretch in all directions. The grass is kept short by
sheep raised in this pasture; they leave telltale piles of dung hidden among the
grass. The pasture is enclosed with a sturdy wooden fence. In the northwest
corner of this enclosure, there is a small shelter for the herdsmen who tend
these sheep.
name: Fenced Green Pasture

desc:
A simple wooden shelter stands here as a resting place for animals bred in the
surrounding pastures. It was built from rough wooden logs and planks, and one of
its walls is open to a pasture to the south. An old, hollowed trunk used as a
watering trough stands in one of the corners, and a hay rack in another.
name: Wooden Shelter

desc:
Five small, closely spaced hills form a ridge going up to the east. The peaks
are round, covered by grass and clumps of bushes. It is possible to ascend the
ridge but the slopes to the north and south are steep and covered in gravel. The
southern slopes are rocky, unstable and falling down would seem unavoidable if a
climb was to be attempted. There is an eroded peak not far to the east towering
above the foothills.
name: Hill Crest

desc:
A pleasant grassy slope descends from a pass in the hills to the east, towards
lower lands in the south-west. To the north the hills are topped by rocky
outcroppings, and piles of scree lie broken along their base. A low wall of
stone can be seen encircling a paddock to the south.
name: Green Hills

desc:
This narrow pass twists through rugged hills. The northern and eastern walls are
covered in loose rocks, making them too treacherous to climb. Judging from the
numerous tracks winding between the sides of the gorge, this pass is often used
by livestock who live higher up the slopes and graze in the valley when there is
an abundance of grass.
name: Rocky Pass

desc:
A large wooden shack stands to the south. Its position takes advantage of
natural shelter provided by a crag that towers behind it. There are steep,
impassable slopes to the east and south, and a fence along the western edge of
this field. Open pastures stretch away to the north.
name: Before a Wooden Shack

desc:
This tiny path does not seem to get much use. Southward it leads into a valley
wedged into the Tower Hills, while to the north it fades away to be almost
indistinguishable from the surrounding terrain. Several animal bones are
scattered around here with a sheep's skull lying to the side of the path. Low
bushes cover the surrounding slopes and grass prevails as the path goes up into
the hills.
name: Animal Trail

desc:
The trail widens slightly here as it winds its way along the bottom of a valley.
The valley is deep and its slopes too steep to be ascended. Small bushes grow on
both sides of the path and they show clear sign of being set upon by grazing
animals.
name: Animal Trail

desc:
Craggy slopes to the south and west force this narrow valley to bend sharply. A
prior landslide has deposited a large number of small stones onto the path
making travel difficult. A small bush is wedged between large rocks to the
south, barely surviving the rockslide. Next to it, there is a patch of fresh
dirt visible beneath one of the piles of rubble. The valley rises to the east,
where it terminates at a dead end surrounded with hills.
name: Valley Bend between Steep Slopes

desc:
Much of the headwall to the east has crumbled away and sent huge piles of scree
down to the valley below. It is not possible to get a clear view of the bottom
without risking a fall. Fortunately, the path to the west seems to offer an
easier route. A gully to the north prevents travellers from reaching the peak
that lies directly beyond it.
name: Before a Steep Crag

desc:
A large rectangular wall has been built here. The walls are made from loose
stones that have been carefully arranged to fit together without any gaps.
Clearly, this was the work of a skilled hand as the structure has stood the test
of time. Moss grows over the stonework yet it is as secure as the day it was
completed. A sturdy door protects the contents from wild animals.
name: Stone-walled Sheepfold

desc:
Wide-bladed grass carpets the ground underfoot, leaving no trace of the rocks
found elsewhere in the Tower Hills. Occasional patches of berry bushes and
bunches of flowers provide bursts of colour. In some areas, the bushes are
missing leaves and the grass is much shorter, a telltale sign of grazing fauna.
name: Lush Hills

desc:
A small tarn surrounded by green grass and small bushes lies at the bottom of a
depression in the valley floor. The water is cold, dark and churns into foam in
numerous places where streams flow in from surrounding the slopes. Many tracks
are imprinted on the banks, showing that a diverse range of wildlife lives here.
A rocky hill towers to the west; its eastern slope invites a climb up.
name: Tarn in the Hills

desc:
Dislodged boulders and mounds of rubble at the bottom of this valley are clear
evidence of a massive landslide. A headwall to the west has collapsed, spreading
layers of gravel and scree down the slope. The loose surface provides no
traction whatsoever, and climbing that way is impossible. The valley that runs
from north to south is far more forgiving for the average traveller.
name: At a Scree Deposit

desc:
Densely packed clumps of large bushes break up the grass that dominates this
sloping valley. The bushes flourish in the fertile soil and have grown so thick
that they block the way in many places. An eroded ridge rises up from the valley
to the west and the valley narrows into a pass to the south.
name: Sloping Valley

desc:
Clumps of bushes blanket a small, narrow valley. The valley descends to the
west, and is closed in by rocky slopes to the east. Luscious grass and numerous
flowers grow among the bushes. The ground is very moist, and sags slightly under
any weight. Numerous large rocks broken off from the surrounding slopes have now
partially sunk into the damp ground of the valley floor.
name: Bushy Grove at the Base of a Rocky Slope

desc:
This small hut consists of one main room with a storage space in its rear. The
main room is furnished with a wooden table, two chairs, and a simple bed with a
pillow and a blanket. A pile of chopped wood sits next to a small fireplace in
one corner of the room. The storage space has a large wooden shelf along with a
number of hooks.
name: Inside a Hunter's Hut

desc:
This den is simply a dark, narrow tunnel hidden under the cover of a cluster of
bushes. The walls are solid and dry, and are covered with large claw marks. The
tunnel provides shelter against adverse weather conditions, making a secure
hideaway for its inhabitants.
name: Den

desc:
Two ridges meet here, coming together to form a rocky, eroded peak. From atop
this peak, the entire western countryside, all the way to the gulf of Lhun, is
visible. This would be an ideal place to observe the coming and going of others
in the fields and hills below.
name: Eroded Peak

desc:
Rocky dirt, combined with a lack of moisture, makes life very difficult here.
The land is almost completely devoid of animals and the only plant life is dry,
struggling shrubs with a few insects buzzing around them. The bluff ends in a
vertical drop to the north, casting a deep shadow over the orchard at its foot.
The slopes to the west and east are steep, but it is possible to climb them with
some effort. A long, finger-like ridge descends southward from the bluff to the
fields below.
name: Edge of a Bluff

desc:
The ground is strewn with rocks and gravel; the only vegetation is tough bushes
and a few clumps of struggling trees. The safest way off the ridge would be to
follow it until its end; either northward, where it levels out at the top of a
bluff, or southward where it descends to merge with the grasslands. However, it
is possible to climb down the eastern slope with some effort.
name: Inclining Crest

desc:
Runoff from nearby hills collects here in small mossy depressions. A coppice of
willows has established itself here to take advantage of the abundance of water.
There are willows at all stages of development, from venerable old trees with
densely intertwined canopies to newly cut stumps with fresh new shoots. Brambles
and woodland flowers surround the stumps, soaking up the abundant light. There
is a mound of willow logs set up for charcoal burning in a clearing atop an
elevated patch of ground.
name: Willow Coppice

desc:
A small trail winds through meadows covered with grass and clusters of flowers
in bloom. Wide rows of tall shrubbery runs across the meadows, following shallow
creeks across the fields. Birds are ever present, flitting from bush to bush,
and the air is filled with twittering. There is a stone wall to the west around
a garden and a small cottage. The Tower Hills rise in low, rugged rows far to
the southeast.
name: Trail through Meadows

desc:
White and yellow flowers surround a patch of bushes in the middle of luxuriant
grassland. To the south, the ground slopes upward, forming the foothills of the
Tower Hills. Fences have been erected to the east, preventing travel in that
direction. There is a small, wet depression to the west.
name: Verdant Grassland

desc:
A narrow, grassy valley cuts between two rows of hills that rise steadily to the
south. A tall peak rises above the ridge to the west, crowned by a grey stone
tower. The fertile land nestled between these heights is remarkably fertile,
sustaining abundant wildlife.
name: Valley in Rising Foothills

desc:
This hilltop offers a clear, unbroken view of a verdant valley that opens into
plains to the northwest, which themselves lead eventually to the faint,
shimmering blue of the Gulf of Lhun. A small wooden shelter is tucked under the
base of a crag to the north. A faint, narrow path follows a ridge to the south,
climbing eventually to reach the top of a towering peak. A gentler ascent to the
west descends into a long valley.
name: Hill Overlooking the Plains

desc:
A grey stone tower soars atop the summit of this hill, the first significant
peak when the range is approached from the west. The tower sits upon a cap of a
grey rock overlooking a cliff to the north. The only way down is a steep path to
the east or a narrow path following a ridge to the south. To the east and south,
the Tower Hills undulate wildly like waves in a storm. Not far to the west,
farmland and a small river stretch out below the hills like a carpet. Far to the
west the land recedes beneath the blue-grey expanse of the sea.
name: Peak over the Plains

desc:
A prominent mound on the western slope is the only noteworthy feature of this
otherwise unremarkable hill. Rocky patches among the heather have been worn
smooth by wind and rain. Small bones and scraps of fur on one such bare patch
show that birds of prey have been active in the area.
name: Small Hillock

desc:
Steep slopes flank a narrow pass, a stark contrast to rolling hills that fall
away to the north. A sharp ridgeline climbs to the east, continuing deeper into
the Tower Hills. A broad, stable slope descends westward. There is little in the
way of vegetation; there are only a few pine trees and patches of yellow grass
on the rocky, barren slopes.
name: Pass between Steep Slopes

desc:
A trail climbs through a pine forest covering the slopes of a hill. The way west
is easy as the trail cuts gently across the slope. At the summit of a high hill,
the trail turns northward and climbs up a narrow ridge leading up to a peak to
the north. There is no viable passage to the south as there is a deep, abrupt
crevice in front of a broad, flat-topped hill.
name: Trail through Pine Tree Clad Hills

desc:
The sides of this rounded hill are steep and rocky. Climbing straight to the top
would be very difficult. However, a trail from the farmlands to the north winds
gently up the hill, making for an easier ascent to the top. A small fault on the
western slope slices open the hill to reveal a steep cliff.
name: Steep Trail in the Hills

desc:
Clusters of colourful flowers dot these green fields, and the grass grows wild
and tall. A trail comes from the north and splits here. One branch leads
southwards where it ascends into the Tower Hills and the other crosses the
fields towards a small forested hill to the west. An old tower stands atop a
steep hill to the south.
name: Trail at the Foot of the Hills

desc:
The sound of rushing water from the rapids below is somewhat muffled by a fringe
of trees that leans over the steep slope to the west. The slope is slick with
water and covered in lichen, liverwort and drooping ferns. Away from the edge,
the ground is mostly covered in thin turf, with slabs of white rock forming
islands in the sea of green.
name: Above River Rapids

desc:
Haunches of preserved meat hang from the ceiling of this small shack. The only
light is from a small window in the western wall. Some faded, threadbare rugs
cover the floor. Clearly, this is not the home of a rich person. Jars for herbs
and spices line the shelf over a stove.
name: Shepherd's Home

desc:
This rocky peak towers thirty yards above the nearby hills. The climb up is
reasonably difficult as there are no paths and little traction on the
grass-covered slopes. From the summit, there is a clear view of the foothills
and plains to the north but higher peaks restrict the view to the west. A green
valley cuts from north to south at the base of the hill to the east.
name: Small Peak

desc:
Four sturdy rock walls and a strong wooden gate protect this place from
predators. The ground underfoot is muddy and the last remnants of vegetation
have been worn or chewed away years ago. Despite the pen having no roof, the
smell is strong enough to indicate that it is still in constant use. Dung and
hoofprints cover the ground.
name: Sheepfold

desc:
Heavy erosion has created a shallow cave in the mountainside. The walls are
rough, and crumbled pebbles cover the ground. The cave is only a few yards deep
and high. Numerous bleached bones and crushed skulls litter the ground near the
entrance to the cave. A narrow ledge leads away from the cave, descending to a
rocky ridge.
name: Rocky Shelter

desc:
A path runs along the top of the ridgeline that stretches away to the west of
here. Erosion has left a steep drop on either side removing any hope of leaving
the ridge safely. The ground slopes away to the east where the ridge descends
into a wide valley. Bushes and tall grass grow partway up the slope, as if to
greet the ridge.
name: Narrow Ridge

desc:
The hills here are heavily eroded, leaving steep and impassable sides to the
north and south. Piles of scree lie at the foot of these slopes as testimony to
the dangers of attempting a descent. Only a narrow path along the top of the
ridge provides safe passage through the area. A few trees grow near the ridge,
their crowns almost level with the top of the ridge. To the east, the Tower
Hills dominate the horizon.
name: Narrow Ridge

desc:
The route narrows and climbs to the top of a distinct spiny ridgeline twisting
through rugged, rocky outcrops. Sparse trees dot the slope to the west, cutting
off abruptly where the rising ground exposes this area to the full, unsheltered
effects of the weather. Tufts of yellow grass cower among rocks and in small
hollows.
name: Exposed Ridgeline

desc:
The peak here consists mostly of loose talus with a few wisps of grass vying for
survival amid the rocks. The lack of soil here has left the area bald and devoid
of substantial vegetation, a stark contrast to the fertile valley floor that can
be seen in the distance to the northwest. A steep ridge falls away to the north.
name: Windswept Peak

desc:
The ground here is damp and soft, sinking slightly when tread upon. To the
north, the grass has is flattened where prevailing winds sweep around an old
grey cottage. A small path wraps around the base of a hill covered with trees. A
dense hedge runs along its northern border; a branch of the path leads up to a
gate which blends cleanly into the rest of the hedge.
name: Flattened Grasses

desc:
A narrow path stretches around a steep riverbank to the east, slowly turning
northward and away from the river. The ground around the bank is solid; it is
rocky with only a thin layer of soil. Further from the river, the land becomes
more fertile, covered with wildly growing grass. Although the path does not
continue westward around the bank, many tracks head in this direction.
name: Path along a Riverbank

desc:
A path starts at the backdoor of a farmhouse and leads south over farmland. Dry
stone walls divide the land into manageable fields. To the north, a long wall
runs from the west to the east, enclosing this farmland against the wilder
scrubland around it. The cottage is made from large, pale grey stones inset with
a number of small windows. The woodwork is old and cracked but has been recently
repainted with a coat of blue. Smoke rises from a chimney in the middle of a
dark slate roof.
name: Path by a Back Door

desc:
Whitewashed walls and a roaring fire give this farmhouse kitchen a rustic but
cosy atmosphere. Small windows in the east wall look out over a quaint cottage
garden and the kitchen is thick with the smell of the herbs left drying over the
fireplace. The flagstone floor is scrupulously scrubbed clean but for the muddy
boots by the backdoor.
name: Whitewashed Kitchen

desc:
Dry stone walls border three sides of this garden, protecting it from livestock
in the surrounding fields. A small cottage abuts the western side, completing
the enclosure. Well-worn cobbled paths wander between plots of flowers and
edible plants. A large compost heap and a water trough are tucked away in a
corner of the garden.
name: Large Garden

desc:
A wooden gate, reinforced with solid plates of iron, stands between two massive
trees. The gate has two iron rings attached to it that can be pulled when it
needs to be opened or closed. A corridor through dense walls of vegetation leads
towards the south where it arrives at a square. The ground close to the gate is
paved with grey stones. A small shelter, with a long bench and table inside,
stands under one of the trees.
name: Corridor through Dense Trees by a Wooden Gate

desc:
A small circular clearing is surrounded by a wall of tall, lean ash. Soft, lush,
and bright green moss covers every surface in the clearing, hiding several large
rocks. This moss comes in many varieties which form large mounds several feet
wide. This grove is calm and peaceful in its protected isolation. A few tiny
trails cut through the forest surrounding the clearing. The foot of a rocky
ridge rises to the east.
name: Mossy Clearing in a Small Ash Grove

desc:
A spiral stair leads down from this tower, and a reinforced wooden door at its
foot presumably leads outside. The main room is a living place, providing
comfort to anyone who wants to stay here. The room is heated with a fireplace
built into one wall. Two beds, a table, four chairs, and a wardrobe stand there.
Eight windows are placed around the living room and provide an outstanding view
to the surroundings.
name: Tower Overlooking the Plains

desc:
The upper part of a broad mesa has been heavily eroded, so that it now forms a
labyrinth of rock pillars, crevices, arches, and other spectacular shapes. Most
of them are nine to eighteen feet high, creating many sheltered crannies where
the wind howls. The mesa ends in abrupt slopes to the north and south, while the
slopes to the west and east make for gentler descents.
name: Eroded Peaks

desc:
The river bends sharply as it turns away from the Tower Hills to the east and
flows westward to the sea. The sound of burbling water can be heard coming from
both upstream and downstream, but here the river is deep and silent. A bank
rises almost vertically to the north where the river has cut its outer edge
deeply into the land. A few huge rocks stick out of the bank, which is otherwise
covered with deep, dark and slippery mud. A small beach has formed to the east,
making for a gentle approach to the river.
name: Bend in the Amber River

desc:
A square greensward is surrounded with wooden houses standing among high trees.
The ground is covered with grass, and many flowers grow between paved footpaths.
Most of the buildings are two-story houses, which have frontages covered with
ivy and glazed windows painted with green leaves. The sound of voices carries
from a small house to the east, and a wooden sign hangs over its entrance.
name: The Square of Lhun Village

desc:
The square lies nestled between wooden houses and ancient trees on three sides.
The grass-covered square extends to the north with paved paths lined by flowers
leading between house and away from the square in several directions. Most of
the buildings are two-storey houses, with fronts covered with ivy and glazed
windows painted with green leaves. A big house on the south side attracts
attention. It has two carved wooden columns on either side of massive
double-doors.
name: The Southern End of the Village Square

desc:
A street runs between the houses of the village Lhun. Most of the houses are
hidden under the boughs of towering trees and covered in ivy, vines, and moss.
Thus coloured doors and glass windows are the only features visible from most of
the buildings. Small gardens open up in front of the houses, sporting bright
flowers, herbs, and other plant life. Gravel footpaths connect the houses to the
street. The twittering of small birds can be heard from above. To the east the
street leads into a square, and to the west it slowly descends to a wicket in a
hedge. Stairs can be seen behind the wicket, leading down around a crag and
ultimately to a small beach of the Amber river.
name: Street Leading to a Beach

desc:
A paved street runs from the west to the east. The street is bordered on both
sides by flowerbeds and wooden houses. A dense forest, full of tall elm trees,
grows beyond the houses. To the east, the street enters a small glade in the
forest. A village square is situated to the west. Cheerful voices and the
clinking of glasses can be heard from a house at the eastern end of the street.
A large sign hangs over the entrance.
name: Street by the Amber Inn

desc:
This large wooden platform sits on the branches of a massive tree. A palisade
surrounds the borders of the platform, presumably both for safety and
protection. There is a trapdoor in the middle of the platform, with a rope
ladder attached to iron rings next to it. It is possible to see a path
approaching this forest to the north, through gaps in the canopy, and a gate
just below the platform. A skilled archer could effectively defend the gates in
times of trouble.
name: Platform in a Treetop

desc:
This place is filled with racks on both sides of the room. The variety of goods
is extensive with vegetables such as garlic, potatoes, and onions standing out
among them. There is a large window on the front side of the store, just next to
the front door. The backside of the room is lit by a row of crystal balls that
are hanging just under the ceiling. A wooden counter stands at the back wall. A
door leading to the inner parts of the house is right by the counter.
name: Store of the White Swan

desc:
A short corridor leads from the front doors to a set of narrow wooden stairs
leading up to a garret. There are two other doors in the corridor. A small
storeroom lies to the right and a bedroom is to the left. The storeroom is full
of fishing nets, rods, and other necessary tools. There is an oven close to the
doors in the living room, heating the whole house. A wooden table with four
chairs stands near the oven under the front windows. There is a small garden
behind the house, visible through a small window at the back of this room.
name: Fisher's House

desc:
The entrance hall is a square room about ten yards wide and is lit by a
chandelier shaped as four swans with each of them holding a light-radiating
crystal ball. There are two high windows on both sides of the massive
doubledoors on the front side of the house. A staircase stands on the opposite
side of the room, leading to the upper floors. A sofa, armchairs, and a table
stand on one side of the stairs with a chair and a desk on the other.
name: Tall Noble House

desc:
The main room of the inn is lit by crystal balls hanging from the ceiling. A
long, solid oak bar runs along the western wall of this room. The aroma of
cooking wafts in through doors behind the bar that likely lead to a kitchen.
Stairs at the back of the room lead upward to the next floor, and there is a
large roaring fireplace across from the bar. The rest of the place is full of
tables and chairs. Paintings of remarkably tall trees hang on the walls.
name: Amber Inn

desc:
Lush, green grass covers a small round glade surrounded by tall, healthy elms.
Their dense, interwoven canopy acts as a natural roof protecting this place from
the vagaries of the weather. There is a path among the trees on the western side
of the glade that leads towards a village street. Hoofprints cover the ground,
especially dense near a natural spring beneath one of the trees.
name: Glade among Towering Elms

desc:
Large fish dart about under the surface of this dark river. Foam crashes against
the west bank as the river slowly turns to the north. A small beach has formed
at the bend; it is covered in a diverse assortment of pebbles. The bed of the
river is composed of these same pebbles, but is too deep and unsteady for
creatures to ford with ease. To the north, the current deepens at another bend
in the river.
name: Gradual Bend in a River

desc:
The sound of rushing water echoes off the steep banks of a narrow river. The
rapids calm where water crashes into a rocky bank to the north that towers five
yards above the surface. The river's flow slows as it turns to the west where
its channel deepens. The full fury of the rapids churns upstream to the south,
where several sharp rocks poke up through the surface of the turbulent waters.
White clumps of foam sail down the dark river, occasionally becoming trapped in
eddies where they form large, spinning whorls.
name: Rapids under a Rocky Bank

desc:
A five yard wide landing protrudes from the western wall of a large hall. There
is a portal in the wall, and the top of a spiral staircase is visible behind it.
The hall extends to the north, east, and south. It might once have been a mighty
hall where an entire tribe of dwarves could gather. However, at some point in
the past much of the hall was destroyed or rebuilt. In many places, there are
great gaps in the floor that reveal a deep chasm beneath this hall. Isolated
islands of remaining floor stand on majestic pillars that rise from the gloom
below. These islands are connected by narrow footbridges. A fall into the
darkness would certainly be lethal. It is difficult to estimate how dangerous
these bridges and platforms are, but a skilful person might be able to determine
their stability. There are faint runes carved into the floor of the landing.
name: Landing on the Western Wall of a Column Hall

desc:
A five yard wide landing protrudes from the eastern wall of a large hall. The
hall extends to the north, south, and west. All that remains of the floor are
scattered stand-alone platforms supported by columns and joined by narrow
footbridges. Massive metal doors in the middle of the eastern wall face onto
this landing. The doors seem to radiate magic, and appear to be both too heavy
to move and too sturdy to break through.
name: Landing on the Eastern Wall of a Column Hall

desc:
This square room is situated between two portals with massive metal doors. These
doors seem to radiate magic. Runes are engraved on them, some glowing faintly;
the doors look impossible to break through and too heavy to be moved. There are
no handles or keyholes on the doors. However, there is a big metal lever
situated in the middle of the room. It protrudes from the floor in a narrow
groove.
name: Between Massive Metal Doors

desc:
A twenty yard corridor with a square cross section connects massive metal doors
on its western end to a deep pit on its eastern end. The corridor is paved with
large marble tiles which vary in shade from white to dark grey. The pit occupies
the entire width of the corridor on the eastern end; its walls are extremely
smooth, making it folly to attempt to climb them.
name: Corridor by Massive Metal Doors

desc:
Four broad corridors meet at this underground junction. The western corridor
ends after a few yards beneath a steep shaft with unclimbable walls. The other
corridors lead into murky darkness to the north, south, and east. Deep,
unnatural scratches mar the stone walls of the corridors.
name: Crossroad beneath a Steep Shaft

desc:
A long, wide hall with a high ceiling extends to the east. The floor is paved
with beautiful marble tiles and the walls are so smooth that they look polished.
Sarcophagi stand along the walls and the scene of a large battle is carved into
the rock behind them. There is a tunnel leading out of the hall to the south and
to either side of it there is a wide niche.
name: Western End of a Tomb

desc:
This cavern is natural in origin, with a few stalactites hanging from its
ceiling. The floor is mostly level, though two large niches appear to have been
carved into the rock. The niches are perfectly square and smoothly polished, and
seem oddly vacant. There is a slightly smaller, more narrow niche on the
opposite end of the room. This niche is not as well crafted as the twin niches
opposite it. The walls of this chamber are made of various types of rock; the
bottom layer is mostly granite, with a layer of quartz above that, finally
ending at a limestone ceiling. Two tunnels exit this cavern, leading east and
west.
name: Small Cavern

desc:
Three corridors meet at this crossroad. Though the walls of the corridors are
rough and uneven, their uniform width and height suggest that they were not
naturally made. The floor is covered with large black and green granite tiles,
one of which is cracked. There are two deep niches in the eastern wall.
name: Crossroad of Stone Corridors

desc:
A long, wide hall with a high ceiling extends to the west. The floor is paved
with beautiful marble tiles and the walls are smooth and polished. Sarcophagi
stand along the walls and a scene depicting a big battle is carved into the rock
behind them. There are some runes on the eastern wall. A tunnel leads out of the
hall to the south.
name: Eastern End of a Tomb

desc:
A long, wide hall with a high ceiling extends to the east and to the west. The
floor is paved with beautiful marble tiles. There are two large sarcophagi along
the northern wall, resting atop pedestals. There is a beautiful and detailed
carving on the wall behind them. The slightest sound made in this tomb resonates
through the halls, bouncing back and forth between the walls.
name: Central Area of a Tomb

desc:
Deep fissures scar the hills, exposing twisted layers of rock in a remarkable
display of orange, black, brown, and red layers. Flecks of mica and quartz
reflect even the faintest of lights, twinkling like a million stars buried deep
inside the hills. Only a small amount of vegetation has been able to find
sufficient soil to take root here.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The road makes a smooth, graceful curve around a tall hill. From here, it
extends to both the north and east. The road's surface is heavily worn, although
there are signs that it has recently been repaired. Thin weeds wearily push
their way through the scant soil beside the road.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Very little vegetation clings to these barren, rocky slopes. Naked stone in hues
of brown and pale grey dominate the landscape, rising and descending in a series
of miniature ridges. The shallow valleys are filled with stones broken free from
the hillsides, from tiny sharp flecks to massive boulders and every size in
between.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
Straight and smooth, the road traverses the landscape, running from the east to
the west. You spot a large tree on the south side of the road, providing shade
for travellers who wish to rest their tired legs. To the north a small lake can
been seen, glimmering through the light vegetation.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Low bushes grow on the sides of the road that runs east-west here. On the
eastern horizon the great Tower Hills reach for the sky. North of here you can
see a small lake and to the south rolling grassy hills fill your view.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The road turns here, continuing north towards the sea and to the east towards
the Shire. Some small trees grow along the road, along with some large thorny
bushes. To the west vast plains spread out but to the south the terrain becomes
rough.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The road bends south and west here. Along the road small green bushes and some
low trees grow. Water can be seen to the north and east. Further east and north
you see the feet of the famous Tower Hills.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Here the road runs from the east to the west, making travel fast and easy.
Behind the large bushes to the north, the hillside is so steep that travel in
that direction is impossible. To the south some fields can be seen.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The road bends north and east here. An occasional tree grows along the road
among large thorny bushes. To the northeast the impressive Tower Hills can be
seen while to the west and south large fields spread out.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Five-foot stalks of golden wheat ripple in gentle sea breezes. The ears are
heavy and full of grain, promising a good harvest. The field is protected by the
steep slope of a bluff to the east and a long fence that encloses it in all
other directions. Deep ruts in the ground lead to a gate in the western fence.
name: Wheat Field under a Bluff

desc:
Very dense bushes have grown large in the shadow of two buildings. A shelter,
smelling of sheep, to the east and another building to the north block these
directions. The soil here is relatively dry, as are the thick and dark green
leaves of the bushes.
name: A Wild Bush behind a Shelter

desc:
Large rose-bushes have grown inmidst the other dense vegetation. The branches of
the old plants are all thick, gnarled and woody, yet the hard thorns of the
ancient rose add even more to the uncomfortable flavour. The remains of an old
nest, probably from more than two years past, can be seen between the dark
leaves. The dry, often sandy soil is covered with stones and a few bleached
bones from a deceased pigeon.
name: Thorny Bushes

desc:
The bushes' leaves are very dense on the outside, but the inside of these old
plants resembles more woody branches than green vegetation. The result is that
looking through these bushes is not much obstructed, yet passage through the by
years hardened and thickened wood is such much harder.
name: Dense Bushes behind a House

desc:
This narrow, dark, and dry cavern goes straight and deep into the rock. The
floor is littered with ravaged carcasses of the unfortunate animals that have
become prey to the predators that call this place home. The decaying corpses
fill the air with the scent of their decay. Some of the rocks forming the
northern wall appear to have been moved recently.
name: Bears' Den
